summary: Joel Spolsky critiques a Jakob Nielsen article on retaining key staff, finding it devoid of actionable content despite multiple readings. He notes that Nielsen's piece merely lists approaches and dismisses them without offering alternatives. Spolsky then redirects readers to his own writings on attracting and managing programmers, as well as the book Peopleware, which he considers the definitive resource on the topic. The post is essentially a brief rebuttal paired with a curated reading list.
key_points: Jakob Nielsen's article on staff retention is criticized for listing ideas only to assert they don't work, without offering solutions
Spolsky positions his own articles on attracting and managing programmers as superior alternatives
Peopleware is recommended as the essential book on managing technical staff, with Spolsky claiming managers who follow it have virtually no turnover
money_quotes: I read the article again and again and it just doesn't say anything.
All it does is list a bunch of things and then asserts that they don't work.
Managers who understand and implement the philosophies of Peopleware have virtually no turnover.
key_insight: Advice on retaining talent is worthless if it only enumerates what doesn't work without offering a constructive alternative.
